facilities.notes: Part of the $24.7B PW Digital Gateway corridor near Manassas National Battlefield Park; rezoning was voided by a Virginia circuit court in August 2025 and upheld by the Court of Appeals in April 2026, leaving the project in legal limbo. Compass's share alone would require up to 1.7 GW at full buildout across ~825 acres.

QTS's ~800-acre portion of the PW Digital Gateway mega-campus on Pageland Lane adjacent to Manassas battlefield; rezoning was voided by Virginia appellate courts in 2025–2026 and remains in legal jeopardy pending a potential Virginia Supreme Court appeal.

145-acre campus along the I-95 corridor with three planned 300 MW substations and up to eight hyperscale buildings totaling 3.5 million sq ft; first substation targeted 150 MW by Q4 2025 with phased delivery through 2027.

$2B, three-building campus (VA4) on 82 acres on Centreport Parkway near Fredericksburg; groundbroken November 2025 with first building expected late 2027, bringing Vantage's total Virginia capacity to 782 MW.

$3B hyperscale campus (VA1) on 87.5 acres groundbroken in Q4 2025; phased delivery of 300 MW in 2027, 300 MW in 2030, and 300 MW by 2033–2035, with 100% renewable energy from an on-site substation and AI-ready closed-loop water cooling.

500-acre campus acquired from Peterson Companies for $302M in January 2025, divided into four sub-campuses with six planned 300 MW Dominion Energy substations; construction expected to begin late 2025 with first phase live by 2027.

The world's premier internet exchange point — Equinix's Ashburn campus comprises 20+ IBX buildings (DC1–DC22) handling over 70% of global internet traffic; anchor of 'Data Center Alley' and the densest colocation/peering ecosystem on earth.

$165M Plaza 500 hyperscale project in the Bren Mar/Bren Pointe residential district of Fairfax County; approved by the county Board of Supervisors in September 2024 but opposed by the 'Save Bren Mar' coalition; as of early 2025 still awaiting Virginia SCC ruling on a required new substation and transmission lines 60 feet from townhomes.

DataBank's IAD5 campus in the Culpeper Technology Zone — an 85-acre, two-facility campus totaling 1.4M sq ft and 192 MW, designed with HPC/AI-ready universal data halls; the CTZ's pro-development zoning framework was designed to preemptively address community opposition.

$3B, 300 MW, 1.525M sq ft data center campus approved 3-2 by the Powhatan County Planning Commission in October 2024 after months of local opposition citing noise, traffic, and environmental impact; Dominion Energy must still build a new substation and power lines, creating an additional battleground for opponents.

Google's original hyperscale campus in Oregon, now comprising multiple buildings with over $2.4 billion invested. Highly controversial for water consumption — Google uses roughly a third of The Dalles' total water supply, prompting the city to seek a reservoir expansion in Mount Hood National Forest.

Google's fifth data center in The Dalles — a 290,000 sq ft facility at 3500 River Road (former aluminum smelter Superfund site) with Whiting-Turner as contractor on a $600 million project. A second building at this site is expected to come online in 2026.

Meta's first wholly owned hyperscale campus, now spanning multiple buildings with an estimated 1,289 MW capacity and over $2 billion invested. The facility uses 100% non-potable water for cooling and helped launch the Open Compute Project.

AWS's flagship Oregon US-West region anchor campus in the Port of Morrow industrial park, supporting multiple buildings since ~2011. AWS has since expanded into Hermiston and Umatilla County with dozens of additional facilities across northeastern Oregon.

Two AWS data centers under active construction in Hermiston as of late 2025, with the city annexing an additional 800 acres zoned for 'hyperscale data center' use to attract further expansion. Local officials raised concerns about water supply and electricity rate hikes.

Amazon purchased 1,300 acres (formerly Threemile Canyon Farms dairy land) along the Columbia River near Boardman in March 2026 for a potential 'exascale' campus of up to 20 buildings, estimated at $12 billion. Oregon's Department of Land Conservation and Development has flagged the unprecedented scale of farmland rezoning needed.

Flexential's fifth Hillsboro data center is a 358,000 sq ft, 36 MW colocation facility under construction and expected to come online in 2026, adding to the company's existing ~113 MW campus that serves as a transpacific interconnection hub.

Flexential acquired a parcel at 3935 NE Aloclek Place in September 2025 for its sixth Hillsboro data center — a 350,000 sq ft, 27 MW facility designed for AI and cloud workloads, located directly across from its Hillsboro 1 campus.

Crane Data Centers broke ground in July 2025 on a 48 MW (two-phase, 24 MW each) campus at 3975 Heather St in Forest Grove, the city's first data center. Residents have raised concerns about noise, property values, wetland impacts, and the height of planned 75-foot buildings.

Sabey purchased 60 acres in Umatilla to develop a 714,540 sq ft, 100+ MW multi-tenant hyperscale campus targeting net-zero carbon by 2029, with ground-breaking expected in early 2025. The facility targets proximity to West Coast subsea cables and Columbia River hydropower.

Project Matador: a 5,769-acre 'HyperGrid' campus co-developed with the Texas Tech University System, targeting 11 GW at full build-out powered by natural gas, solar, and planned Westinghouse AP1000 nuclear reactors; first 1 GW of gas-fired power expected by end of 2026. Faces intense community opposition over air quality (TCEQ approved 93 gas turbines), Ogallala Aquifer strain, and greenhouse gas emissions; subject of a class-action investor lawsuit and Sierra Club warnings that its 5,100+ MW gas capacity would be the largest gas plant in the US.

Meta broke ground in October 2025 on a 1,000-acre hyperscale AI campus in Northeast El Paso near Stan Roberts Sr. Ave.; investment dramatically expanded from $1.5B to $10B in March 2026, with 1 GW capacity targeted by 2028. Faces significant community opposition over water usage (up to 1.5M gallons/day at full build-out), air pollution from 813 modular gas generators, and large property tax abatements; El Paso organized community meetings through April 2026 in response to public backlash.

facilities.operator: PowerHouse Data Centers / Provident Data Centers
facilities.notes: Joint-venture 768-acre hyperscale campus in northern Ellis County between Dallas and Fort Worth, adjacent to Google's Midlothian complex; planned across 24 buildings in three phases with a 1.8 GW switchyard on-site and first building energization targeted for May 2026. The first 500 MW tranche has ERCOT approval; total direct construction impact is estimated at over $5B.

The U.S. Army selected Carlyle Group to develop hyperscale AI data centers on approximately 1,384 acres at Fort Bliss under a 50-year enhanced-use lease, with a targeted $2B investment and initial operations in 2027; the Army retains land ownership while Carlyle finances, builds, and operates. This is the first Texas execution of a 2025 executive order directing underutilized federal land toward commercial AI infrastructure; Army Corps of Engineers will oversee environmental review.

Tract's Caldwell Valley campus grew to nearly 3,000 acres in January 2026 after adding 1,458 acres to an existing 1,515-acre parcel between Austin and San Antonio; designed for up to 2 GW of total capacity with initial 360 MW targeting energization in 2028 under a Facility Design Agreement with Bluebonnet Electric Cooperative. Land acquisition included an 18-month engagement with state agencies and local communities.

Scotland-based Eneus Energy (RGV Property LLC) is planning a 1,785-acre hyperscale campus near Valley International Airport outside Harlingen, with up to 16 data halls and 1 GW of interconnection capacity secured; as of April 2026, no formal proposal has been submitted to local authorities. Cameron County commissioners passed a resolution in April 2026 opposing data centers that lack safeguards, while Harlingen residents raised water and electricity concerns; the city-owned utility agreed to supply over 4.6 million gallons/day of reclaimed wastewater for the project.

CyrusOne's 400 MW data center along the Brazos River in rural Bosque County northwest of Waco is already under construction and projected to increase county tax revenue by 120% (~$70M) over three decades. Community members gathered in strong opposition at town hall meetings in December 2025, raising concerns about noise, water, and the rush of rural data center development; Hood County commissioners rejected a moratorium on new development despite similar public opposition.

Skybox is pursuing a rezoning of approximately 29.69 acres near East Old Settlers Boulevard and A.W. Grimes Boulevard in Round Rock to build a data center adjacent to the Chandler Creek neighborhood; a second campus of ~140 acres to the west is also in planning. The proposal passed its first City Council reading in late 2025; residents raised concerns about noise, water, and energy consumption; a closed-loop cooling system is required under the proposed PUD.

A 200 MW, 1.5 million sq ft colocation campus on West Military Drive and Loop 1604 in San Antonio comprising five buildings; an on-site 334 MW CPS Energy dual-fed substation is included, with the first building targeting 300,000 sq ft of space. The campus is designed to support both air- and liquid-cooled AI workloads with 2N power configuration per building.

Data City is a 5 GW data center campus planned near Laredo, with the first 300 MW and one million square feet of space targeting launch in 2026; the campus could ultimately expand to over 15 million square feet of leasable space. It uses a closed-loop chiller system designed to minimize water consumption, and is pursuing LEED certification.

Known as 'Project Excalibur,' this Blackstone-owned QTS mega-campus spans 615 acres with up to 16 buildings and 6.6 million sq ft; construction began in 2023 with full build-out targeted by 2032. Poised to be one of the world's largest multi-tenant data center campuses.

Highly controversial 'Project Sail': a $17B, 9-building campus on 832 acres of rural/forested land near Plant Yates; rezoning approved in a narrow 3-2 vote in April 2026 despite 8,000+ resident signatures in opposition and concerns over water, noise, and environmental impact. Construction expected to start 12–18 months after land purchase.

'Project Bunkhouse' is a $19B, 8.6 million sq ft, 12-building campus on 876 acres of agricultural land in Bartow County, managed by Digital Realty; rezoning was recommended by the Planning Commission in June 2025. At 1,830 MW full build-out it would consume more power than the entire city of Atlanta.

DC BLOX's 'Atlanta West' hyperscale campus on 100 acres adjacent to Microsoft's 300-acre facility in Douglas County; closed $1.15B in green loan financing in August 2025. Greystone Power Cooperative is the utility provider with an additional 80 MW campus expansion planned for 2027.

DC BLOX's 'Atlanta East' hyperscale campus on 72 acres in Rockdale County, 24 miles east of Atlanta; broke ground September 2024 with a 30 MW and 80 MW building in the first phase. Campus will support up to 144 MW critical load at full build-out.

Manulife-backed Serverfarm is converting a 498,960 sq ft warehouse at 10835 Hazelbrand Rd into a 60 MW hyperscale data center initially powered by natural gas until Georgia Power completes a new substation. The site was annexed into the city of Covington via a 5-1 council vote amid carbon-emissions concerns.

T5 Data Centers finalized a land acquisition in South Fulton County in August 2024 for its 'Atlanta IV' campus, planned to support up to 200 MW of critical IT load across approximately 1.32 million sq ft. The project is in pre-construction planning as of early 2026.

Filed under 'Digital Fort Gillem LLC,' Digital Realty is planning a two-building, 200 MW campus spanning 97 acres and 1.9 million sq ft just nine miles south of Atlanta near the former Fort Gillem military base.

Trammell Crow is planning a $21B 'Forsyth Technology Campus' on a 1,632-acre site along I-75 roughly 60 miles southeast of Atlanta, with 12 million sq ft of data center space across multiple buildings. An early-stage DRI filing was submitted in late 2025; rezoning and community review are pending.

DataBank's Lithia Springs Campus (ATL5/ATL6) sits on 95 acres with a 180 MW on-site Georgia Power substation, targeting 400,000+ sq ft of AI-oriented data center space. The campus is strategically located adjacent to other hyperscale operators in the Douglas County corridor.

Hyperscale campus in Mesa's Elliot Road Technology Corridor expanding to 450+ MW across 6 buildings; 206 MW operational since April 2023 with two additional buildings (PH02/PH03) under construction expected in 2025–2026. Powered by Salt River Project (SRP) with an air-cooled, closed-loop chilled water system requiring near-zero water.

Controversial 290-acre data center campus on unincorporated Pima County land near Houghton Road, developed by Beale Infrastructure (Blue Owl-backed). Tucson City Council unanimously rejected annexation in August 2025; Pima County Board approved land sale 3-2 in December 2025 and land closed Dec. 24, 2025. ACC approved a TEP energy supply agreement (4-1) in December 2025; AG Kris Mayes contested the energy deal. Originally tied to Amazon Web Services; sources link Meta as a possible end-user after AWS exit. Faces active litigation from the No Desert Data Center Coalition and ongoing opposition over energy rates, water use, and lack of public transparency. Target operational date 2027.

Proposed 600-acre data center campus by Beale Infrastructure (same developer as Project Blue) in the Town of Marana; rezoning approved by the Town Council in January 2026. Projected $5 billion capital investment and 550–750 MW of power at full buildout. Faces strong community opposition over noise from backup generators, energy rate impacts, and proximity to residential areas; the No Desert Data Center Coalition is actively organizing against it.

Two ~1-million-sq-ft data center buildings on a ~160-acre unincorporated Maricopa County site just north of Luke Air Force Base, powered by an on-site 700 MW natural gas plant (ACC issued Certificate of Environmental Compatibility unanimously Feb. 4, 2026). Construction slated Q3 2026; first building online Q1 2028. Faces intense grassroots opposition (Stop Project Baccara Coalition, ~5,000-signature petition) over air quality from gas generators, residential proximity, water well impacts, and carbon emissions.

Massive $33 billion 'La Osa Project' on a 3,374-acre site in unincorporated Pinal County along the Greene Canal south of Eloy, capable of up to 3 GW—more than the entire Phoenix metro's current data center capacity. Pinal County Planning & Zoning held a non-voting informational session in July 2025; commissioners raised concerns about flood zones, land subsidence fissures, wildlife corridors, and feasibility. Energy plan begins with a gas-fired plant transitioning to solar; air-cooled design proposed. No hyperscale tenant confirmed. Rezoning approval anticipated 2026; full buildout over ~10 years.

Developer Anita Verma-Lallian (backed by Chamath Palihapitiya) purchased the ~2,100-acre Hassayampa Ranch site for $51 million in May 2025 and received Maricopa County Board of Supervisors zoning approval for a data center industrial park estimated at $25 billion. Up to 6–8 hyperscalers reportedly interested, including Meta, Google, and OpenAI. Rural residents in Tonopah opposed the project over water well impacts, property values, noise, rising energy costs, and loss of rural character.

Aligned Data Centers broke ground April 15, 2025 on its Glendale campus (PHX-13), the first of four planned facilities on a 100-acre site totaling 72 MW for this phase. Powered by a new 230 kV APS transmission line; uses Aligned's Delta³ air cooling arrays with a closed-loop water recycling system to minimize consumption in the arid Southwest.

AZP-3 is the third data center on Iron Mountain's 39-acre Phoenix campus (joining operational AZP-1 and AZP-2), featuring 36 MW of IT load supported by 21 emergency generators, precast insulated concrete construction, and roof-mounted mechanical chillers. Expected completion May 2026.

Google's Redhawk Mesa campus at the intersection of Elliot and Sossaman roads spans ~750,000 sq ft at full buildout; Phase 1 (288,530 sq ft data hall) became operational July 2025. Phase 2 targets 280,000 sq ft and 400 MW across 185 acres and is under construction. Supports Google Cloud and AI services.

Stream's 157-acre Phoenix hyperscale campus (seven buildings, PHX I–VII) near Goodyear is the largest data center development of its kind in the Valley, targeting 280 MW of critical load across 2+ million sq ft at full buildout. PHX I (40 MW) is fully leased and operational; PHX II and subsequent buildings are under development for hyperscale tenants.

Meta's largest global data center campus, spanning 520 acres and over 2.7 million sq ft (expanding to 5 million sq ft), with a total investment exceeding $2.5 billion; powered entirely by renewable wind energy and employs ~400 on-site.

Microsoft's Project Alluvion campus at 550 SE White Crane Road is the largest single data center in Iowa at ~200 MW and over 918,000 sq ft; part of a multi-campus West Des Moines cluster where Microsoft has invested $3–5 billion and owns 650+ acres.

Apple's 2,000-acre Waukee campus (Project Morgan) opened its first building in 2024 and is expanding toward a planned six-building, ~1.9 million sq ft campus with an investment of ~$1.3–1.4 billion; runs on 100% renewable energy.

QTS is constructing a $750 million–$1.75 billion, 612-acre, 7-building campus at the Big Cedar Industrial Center in SW Cedar Rapids; uses a waterless cooling system and is the largest economic development investment in Cedar Rapids history.

Meta (via subsidiary Vemerald LLC) has purchased ~328 acres in north Davenport for an $800 million, 715,000 sq ft two-building campus; as of early 2026 no construction has begun despite an earlier Q4 2025 groundbreaking target, raising concerns about electricity and water impacts.

Google is constructing a $576 million multi-building campus at the Big Cedar Industrial Center as part of a $7 billion Iowa investment; first building expected operational in 2026 with four additional buildings planned in future phases.

Google proposed a six-building campus near the Duane Arnold nuclear plant in Linn County, signing a 25-year nuclear PPA in late 2025; highly controversial due to water and local-control concerns, prompting Linn County to pass a new data center ordinance in February 2026, after which Google pivoted to seek annexation into the City of Palo.

COPT acquired 365 acres in Van Meter in late 2024 for a planned 15-building, ~1 GW, 3.3 million sq ft hyperscale campus targeting an existing Fortune 100 cloud-computing tenant; development is planned in phases on a leverage-neutral basis.
Edged Data Centers announced a $187 million, 13.2 MW, 105,000 sq ft AI-focused data center on 17.5 acres in Ankeny (north of Des Moines); construction began H2 2025 with completion targeted for 2027.

CyrusOne's OCB1 colocation facility at 4700 Gifford Road in Council Bluffs spans 20 acres and 216,000 sq ft (first phase completed 2022) at a ~$60 million investment; a major colocation anchor in the Council Bluffs/Omaha market alongside Google's hyperscale campus nearby.

Microsoft's flagship central Washington campus spans ~270 acres across multiple generations of data center design; a fifth-generation AI-ready facility is actively under development on the expanding site.
Microsoft filed plans for a three-building data center campus on ~100 acres along Malaga Alcoa Highway, with the first building targeted by end of 2025 and up to five buildings possible by 2027.

Microsoft operates existing facilities and is actively developing new data centers in East Wenatchee; the company expects to become one of the largest taxpayers in Douglas and Chelan Counties upon full build-out.

Vantage's WA1 campus in Quincy spans 68 acres with three hyperscale data center buildings (including WA13, opened 2023) totaling 775,000 sq ft and 89 MW of critical IT load, powered by Grant County hydroelectricity.

Sabey's Intergate.Quincy campus spans 525,000 sq ft across five purpose-built buildings; the company completed an 85 MW two-building expansion in 2023 and plans further incremental capacity additions through 2028.

Sabey's Intergate.Columbia campus in East Wenatchee sits on 30 acres and is fully leased; the company is actively expanding the site with approximately 9 MW of additional capacity planned for late 2026.

Amazon Data Services (formerly operating as 'Advance Phase LLC') purchased ~554 acres at Wallula Gap Business Park for a $5 billion, 16-building campus across four phases; the Port of Walla Walla confirmed Amazon as buyer in February 2026.

Equinix SE2 at the landmark Westin Building in downtown Seattle is the state's premier carrier-neutral colocation hub and internet exchange point, serving as the primary interconnection node for Pacific Rim and cloud traffic.
H5's 240,000 sq ft Quincy facility offers up to 40 MW at full buildout, marketed as the lowest-cost power data center market in the US at under $0.03/kWh via Grant County hydropower.

Voltage Park opened an AI-focused data center in Quincy in 2024, citing clean hydroelectric power, state tax incentives, and proximity to Washington state universities as key location factors; the company also operates a facility in Puyallup, WA.

A $10 billion, 800-acre campus at Energy Way Industrial Park featuring up to 20 data center buildings; broke ground October 2025 and is billed as the largest single capital project in North Carolina history. Sited adjacent to Duke Energy's 2.24 GW Sherwood H. Smith Jr. natural gas complex; full build-out expected 2027–2028.

A $1B+ four-campus cluster (Lyle Creek/Conover, Boyd Farm/Maiden, Stover/Hickory) totaling an estimated 720–810 MW across 15 planned 48 MW buildings; construction restarted across all three sites simultaneously in early 2026 after a ~7-month pause. Duke Energy is contracted to supply power.

Apple's flagship iDataCenter campus in Catawba County, operational since 2012 with ~1 million sq ft across four buildings and roughly $4B+ invested to date; a $175M expansion was permitted in 2025 as part of Apple's broader $500B U.S. investment plan. Campus is powered partly by on-site solar arrays and fuel cells.

Google's original North Carolina campus, operational since 2007 in Caldwell County; in March 2026 Google announced a new $1B two-year investment to expand the campus on ~60 adjacent acres. The facility consumed 327 million gallons of water in 2024.

A 156-acre, 400 MW campus at 12899 Moores Chapel Road acquired for $160M; rezoning approved by Charlotte City Council in May 2025 for a 3 million sq ft two-phase build. Site clearing has begun but formal construction start has not been announced.

A $19.2B, 900 MW, 300-acre campus in the Kingsboro development; groundbreaking was targeted for Q1 2026 after Edgecombe County commissioners amended zoning to permit data centers in heavy industrial districts. A separate 100 MW ESS project in Tarboro was previously rejected by the town following community opposition.

facilities.operator: Blue Ridge Companies / Engineered Land Solutions
facilities.notes: Known as 'Project Delta,' a proposed 1,800-acre campus near Duke Energy's Belews Creek plant site targeting several hundred MW; the Stokes County planning board voted against it in December 2025 and ~100 residents protested at the January 2026 commission meeting. An Action Network petition from Clean Water for NC garnered over 2,600 signatures citing Dan River pollution risks.

WhiteFiber's NC-1 campus on 96 acres in Rockingham County, transforming a former industrial site into a 970,000 sq ft AI-optimized facility scaling from 24 MW to potentially 200 MW; Nscale signed an $865M deal for 40 MW with delivery beginning April 2026. Total projected investment exceeds $1B.

A 333–350-acre campus along Stamey Farm Road and Hickory Highway comprising five data center buildings (~270,000 sq ft each) with air-cooling systems; Statesville City Council finalized rezoning in October 2025, and Compass submitted a site plan application. Duke Energy's contracted to supply power via a nearby substation.

facilities.operator: PowerHouse Data Centers (American Real Estate Partners)
facilities.notes: A 122-acre, five-building campus on University City Boulevard designed for up to 1.5 million sq ft; construction began Q1 2025 with 300 MW of power delivery anticipated by Q2 2027 and potential expansion to 400 MW. Part of a broader Duke Energy data-center supply deal announced February 2026.

Groundbreaking held March 20, 2026 on the former Portsmouth Gaseous Diffusion Plant (DOE land), now branded the 'PORTS Technology Campus'; the 10 GW data center campus will be powered by a co-located 9.2 GW natural gas generation complex in a DOE-SoftBank-AEP Ohio public-private partnership. Part of a $550B Japan-US trade commitment.

A $4 billion hyperscale campus spanning 471 acres off US-68, first proposed in late 2025; faces intense community opposition and repeated tabling by the Wilmington Planning Commission due to incomplete traffic, water, and stormwater studies as of March 2026.

A $3.6 billion, 1.65 million sq-ft AI data center campus on 133 acres along SR-45; Lordstown Village Council initially voted 6-0 to ban all data centers before switching to a moratorium after the developer filed a lawsuit with the Ohio Supreme Court demanding zoning compliance.

Dubbed 'Project Klondike,' this ~$1B data center was fast-tracked by the Piqua City Commission in November 2025 via emergency resolution under an NDA; investigative reporting linked shell company J5 LLC to Meta's HQ address, sparking a 2,500+ signature petition from residents over lack of transparency and water/energy concerns.

facilities.operator: DB Stu LLC (linked to Meta Platforms)
facilities.notes: Shell company DB Stu LLC, incorporated the same day as a confirmed Meta subsidiary, is proposing a data center in Mount Orab; officials signed an NDA prohibiting disclosure of the developer's identity, prompting Ohio Rep. Adam Bird to introduce legislation banning NDAs in such public approval processes.

Aligned's third Ohio campus, a multi-building hyperscale AI campus on a 197-acre brownfield adjacent to the former AEP Conesville coal power plant in Coshocton County; initial capacity delivery targeted for mid-2026 with a foundational customer already secured.
Aligned's second Ohio campus, a 129-acre site near Sandusky in Erie County that broke ground in late 2024; planned for four buildings totaling 96 MW at full build-out.

Vantage's OH1 campus spans 70 acres and is planned for three two-story hyperscale data centers totaling 192 MW across 1.5 million sq ft; first building targeted operational by December 2025, with additional phases through 2028. Built to LEED Silver standards.

Edged opened its New Albany data center in early 2026 at 6526 New Albany Road East; the two-story facility delivers 24 MW of capacity across 206,000 sq ft after approximately 13 months of construction.

Former coal plant site converted to AI/HPC campus in Niagara County; Fluidstack (backed by Google, which holds a ~14% stake in TeraWulf) has leased 360 MW across 10-year agreements worth ~$3.7B. CB-4 and CB-5 buildings energization slated for H2 2026, with total infrastructure capacity expandable to 750 MW. Residents near the Somerset facility report constant ~95-decibel noise from cooling fans.

TeraWulf secured an 80-year ground lease on ~183 acres at the former Cayuga Power Plant (Milliken Station, retired 2019) to develop up to 400 MW of data center capacity, with 138 MW expected online in H2 2026. Environmentalists and local critics warn of noise, water use from Cayuga Lake, and disputed green-energy claims, calling it a potential 'sacrifice zone.'

Proposed $19.5B hyperscale campus (backed by Apollo Global Management) at the 1,250-acre STAMP industrial megasite in Genesee County, targeting a Fortune 50 tenant. Faces intense community and legal opposition from the Tonawanda Seneca Nation, Sierra Club, and local residents over wetlands impact, noise, electricity cost hikes, and a controversial $1.46B public subsidy package. GCEDC began reviewing the financial assistance application in February 2026.

Proposed hyperscale campus on 120 acres of vacant land in suburban Lysander (Onondaga County), led by local developer James Ranalli. Has requested 300 MW of grid interconnection from NYISO — equivalent to ~200,000 average households. Currently in early planning stages with no confirmed anchor tenant.

IBM announced in June 2025 a dedicated IBM Quantum Data Center in Poughkeepsie to house its 'Quantum Starling' system — the world's first planned large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computer using 200 logical qubits. Full delivery expected by 2029. Not a conventional hyperscale facility but a landmark quantum-computing infrastructure project.

Equinix's NY/NJ Secaucus campus (NY2/NY3/NY4) is the company's largest site in the New York City metro, serving financial, media, and enterprise customers with dense carrier and cloud interconnection. NY3 is Equinix's largest individual site in the metro and a critical Internet Exchange hub. The campus faces NYC grid pressure and Local Law 97 carbon-intensity compliance requirements.

Digital Realty operates multiple carrier-dense colocation facilities in the NYC metro (including 111 8th Ave and 60 Hudson St area interconnection points), controlling a majority of carrier-dense square footage alongside Equinix and CoreSite. Facilities face NYC Local Law 97 carbon-intensity fines beginning 2025, requiring operators to procure zero-carbon power or electrify mechanical loads.

DataBank's Orangeburg complex, located ~30 miles north of Midtown Manhattan in Rockland County, is an exurban 'clean-sheet' development with approximately 45 MW of expansion headroom. It illustrates the broader trend of carriers moving data center load out of congested NYC to suburban sites with better substation capacity and lower property costs.
